[186:01]where a U.S.-Saudi coalition tried to overthrow Houthi rebels in Yemen's capital.

[186:31]through regime change, Libya, Iraq, Syria, destabilized Sudan, Somalia, and Lebanon.

[186:46]Iran is Israel's last opponent in the Middle East.

[186:59]It has a much larger population.

[187:01]It has a much stronger sense of national identity.

[187:04]It's much older than many of these other polities.

[187:08]It has a strong sense of identity

[187:10]because it is the center of the Shiite religion.

[187:21]It has a domestic defense industry.

[187:32]years.

[187:33]And that means that Iran is pursuing a nuclear energy program.

[187:47]be weaponized, and with their missile and space program, could in short order become

[188:02]They could miniaturize it.

[188:03]They could put it on a missile, and they would have the ability potentially to acquire a nuclear

[188:10]triad of their own.

[188:21]The reason that Iran is developing a nuclear program,

[188:25]I don't want to say an arsenal,

[188:27]but the reason that Iran has pursued this threshold status

[188:30]is because Iran sees what Israel has been doing.

[188:34]Iran sees what Israel and the United States have done over the past 40 years.

[188:39]They see what happened to Lebanon, what happened to Iraq, Libya, Syria, Yemen.

[188:59]Having a nuclear bomb is the only capability

[189:02]that protects you by deterring the United States

[189:06]or some other regional country from going to war with you.

[189:10]But there's a bit of a paradox.

[189:13]If Iran actually acquires a nuclear weapon,

[189:16]that would almost guarantee that the United States

[189:19]might try to intervene just before they acquired one.

[189:34]successfully testing one. That period, there would almost certainly be U.S.

[190:00]why they really maintain both of those capabilities,
